category_summary:
The AI community focused on two dominant stories: **OpenAI's** aggressive Codex pricing play and the **UK AI Security Institute's** alarming findings on frontier model cyber capabilities.

- **Sam Altman** [announced 2 months free Codex](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-f33fd2039ff3) for enterprises, signaling all-out competition for AI coding market share
- **UK AISI** found **Anthropic's Mythos Preview** is the [first model to solve both](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-8a3a31828632) their cyber ranges end-to-end, with a 4.5-month capability doubling time
- **Ethan Mollick** [questioned **Anthropic's** path forward](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-cab5f59ce231) given competitors will release equivalent models without restrictive guardrails
- **Anthropic** [surpassed **OpenAI** in enterprise spending](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-6fe66db70a03) for the first time in April, per **Ramp** data
- **Sam Altman** [hinted at a strategic pivot](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-70bb4cab9409), musing whether **OpenAI** should prioritize speed/cost over raw intelligence

Research and open-source developments also drew attention: an **EMNLP 2025** paper showed competitive reasoning models can be [trained in just 26 minutes](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-b7235b287f9e) with 1,000 examples, while **Hugging Face** [open-sourced a CLI agent](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-a2bab06e3df4) automating full ML research loops. **Clement Delangue** [urged the American AI community](/?date=2026-05-14&category=social#item-5e985e841a25) to support open international collaboration during the Trump-Xi summit.

items.title: This EMNLP 2025 paper shows that a pretrained 32-billion-parameter model can be turned into a compet...
items.content:
This EMNLP 2025 paper shows that a pretrained 32-billion-parameter model can be turned into a competitive reasoning model using only 1,000 carefully chosen examples and 26 minutes of additional training on 16 GPUs.

The authors control how much the model "thinks" before answering through a method they call budget forcing: when the model tries to end its reasoning, they suppress its end-of-thinking token and append the word "Wait," which often nudges it to reconsider and fix earlier mistakes.

With this setup, the model matches OpenAI's o1-preview on competition math and graduate-level science questions, even though comparable open replications like DeepSeek's r1 used roughly 800 times more training examples.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/jdaMm8GZ0x

PDF: https://t.co/zD3MB2IAQA

items.summary: Shares EMNLP 2025 paper showing a 32B parameter model can become a competitive reasoning model using only 1,000 examples and 26 minutes of training on 16 GPUs, matching o1-preview on math/science using 'budget forcing' technique with 800x fewer examples than DeepSeek R1.
items.reasoning: Significant research finding on training efficiency for reasoning models. Burkov is a recognized ML author. The 800x reduction in training data is a notable result with practical implications for democratizing reasoning model training.

items.title: The most expensive mistake in enterprise AI right now: treating FDEs as your whole transformation pl...
items.content:
The most expensive mistake in enterprise AI right now: treating FDEs as your whole transformation plan. 

Forward deployed engineers (FDEs) are important for custom deployments, but they won’t fix the change management issue most enterprises are facing. 

It’s likely more the former that Anthropic and OpenAI will continue to prioritize (and hire into the thousands, who knows). Beyond performance and cost, it’s systems integration, ROI, and literal usefulness that drive revenue and stickiness. 

 *However* 

External FDEs, in my opinion, will not make your company an AI-first company. 

You can have the sleekest multi-agent orchestrations and still have the majority of your employee base hating AI, avoiding AI, and distrusting leadership decisions on AI. 

And we already know this because we see this in traditional SaaS too: you can customize the heck out of your Salesforce deployment, but that doesn’t mean your sales team will improve their data hygiene or even attempt to change the way they track and grow with it. 

Buying a fancier car doesn’t mean you magically learn to drive better overnight. 

If you’re an enterprise exec and FDEs are sold as the immediate and sole solution to your company transformation woes, walk away. 

It’s the combination of tech *and* people enablement *and* process reinvention that compounds into actual business outcomes.

Large complex enterprises will stall out if they only prioritize the first.

Emphasizes that tech deployment without change management, people enablement, and process reinvention will stall. Compares to Salesforce adoption challenges.

items.title:
Hugging Face open-sourced a CLI agent that runs full research loop of an ML engineer. 

You give it ...
items.content:
Hugging Face open-sourced a CLI agent that runs full research loop of an ML engineer. 

You give it a prompt. It reads papers and walks citations.
 
Then it pulls datasets and writes training scripts. 

It launches GPU jobs and iterates until something works.
 
On scientific reasoning, it trained Qwen3-1.7B and pushed GPQA from 10% to 32%. 

Claude Code only reached 22.99%.
 
For healthcare, it judged public datasets too weak. 

So it generated 1,100 synthetic samples and upsampled 50x. 

It beat Codex on HealthBench by 60%.
 
On competitive math, rewards collapsed mid-run. 

The agent ran ablations and recovered the training.
 
Three things make it work:
 
1. Reads papers and citation graphs
2. Reformats messy data before training
3. Diagnoses failed evals autonomously
 
Ships as a CLI and a web app you can run from your phone.

items.title:
Your Agent Can Now Train Models

The argument from @mervenoyann: open source models have caught up. ...
items.content:
Your Agent Can Now Train Models

The argument from @mervenoyann: open source models have caught up. GLM 5.1 is leading the Artificial Analysis intelligence index over closed models, and the gap is closing with every release cycle. Weight access means you can quantize, fine tune, and deploy to edge devices without data leaving your infrastructure.

https://t.co/kQvBd0uHuk

The talk covers the Hugging Face ecosystem built for agentic work: inference providers with tool use routing, benchmark datasets for filtering by SWE bench scores on Hub, a traces repository type for storing agent sessions, and skills that plug into coding agents. The closer is a live demo: she asks Claude Code to fine tune a vision language model on a dataset by name. The agent calculates VRAM requirements, picks an instance, and kicks off the job. What used to be a day of napkin math is now a prompt.

It’s also being redesigned to conduct back-and-forth conversations with a chatbot interface, matching the look of ChatGPT, Gemini and other apps."

New Siri will be shown off at WWDC in June.

Finally.

items.title:
Now that claude/codex is getting better I'm starting to give it much more complicated tasks.

I usua...
items.content:
Now that claude/codex is getting better I'm starting to give it much more complicated tasks.

I usually spend ~1 hour in planning phase. Each turn to update the plan or execute part of a plan now takes ~10-15mins.

Right now I'm driving 4 major tasks in parallel across cursor agents and claude code.

The context switching struggle is becoming a huge issue.

Let this one area of working with AI not be weird. Just ask for stuff, in well-specified formats, like a manager, not a sorcerer with a bunch of incantations.

items.title:
MCP is not dead.

For the people who keep saying "bUt McP pUts GaRbaGe iN yOuR cOntExT", that's an o...
items.content:
MCP is not dead.

For the people who keep saying "bUt McP pUts GaRbaGe iN yOuR cOntExT", that's an outdated complaint:

None of the tools you use suffer from that.

Check Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor: They all implement progressive disclosure and load MCP tools on demand.

In 2026, MCP barely touches your context.

For local agents, a CLI wins as long as there's a filesystem and a shell.

But if you need to reach a cloud-hosted platform that requires authentication and discoverability, nothing beats MCP.

I had to record a video about this.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago defends MCP (Model Context Protocol) against criticism, explaining that modern tools implement progressive disclosure and load MCP tools on demand, making context pollution a solved problem in 2026
items.reasoning: Informative defense of MCP with specific technical points about how leading tools handle it. Relevant to developer ecosystem. Good engagement.

items.title:
just tried this out and it one-shotted* this video: "before the agent does anything"

*i generated t...
items.content:
just tried this out and it one-shotted* this video: "before the agent does anything"

*i generated the narrative using chatgpt and used that as a prompt. featuring: @e2b @runanywhereai @composio @mem0ai @firecrawl @browser_use @agentmail @covenantlabsai

some thoughts:
- i clearly tried to stick too much into 30 seconds, they talk very fast and lost some content which breaks logic
- character consistency is strong, i uploaded a single screenshot from my prior video as reference
- voice consistency was not automatic. you notice unicorn switch from female to male voice part way through
- the agent gives you an editor with generated scenes broken up but i don't see a way to regenerate a single section in the UI (which would be nice)
- it is definitely a much better experience to have the agent stitch videos together than doing it yourself (i was using canva). was trying @flymy_ai's media agent api for it this weekend which also works well and with other models
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei Nakajima reviews Runway Agent: one-shotted a video with character consistency, notes issues with voice consistency, speaking speed, and lack of per-scene regeneration. Compares favorably to manual Canva editing. Also mentions flymy_ai's media agent API.
items.reasoning: Useful first-hand review of Runway Agent from a notable AI builder (BabyAGI creator). Provides practical pros/cons that complement the launch announcement.
items.summary_html: Yohei Nakajima reviews Runway Agent: one-shotted a video with character consistency, notes issues with voice consistency, speaking speed, and lack of per-scene regeneration. Compares favorably to manual Canva editing. Also mentions flymy_ai's media agent API.
items.content_html:
just tried this out and it one-shotted* this video: "before the agent does anything"

*i generated the narrative using chatgpt and used that as a prompt. featuring: @e2b @runanywhereai @composio @mem0ai @firecrawl @browser_use @agentmail @covenantlabsai

some thoughts:

  • i clearly tried to stick too much into 30 seconds, they talk very fast and lost some content which breaks logic
  • character consistency is strong, i uploaded a single screenshot from my prior video as reference
  • voice consistency was not automatic. you notice unicorn switch from female to male voice part way through
  • the agent gives you an editor with generated scenes broken up but i don't see a way to regenerate a single section in the UI (which would be nice)
  • it is definitely a much better experience to have the agent stitch videos together than doing it yourself (i was using canva). was trying @flymy_ai's media agent api for it this weekend which also works well and with other models

Delivering agentic inference at scale requires balancing efficiency across:

1) Models and algorithm...
items.content:
Delivering agentic inference at scale requires balancing efficiency across:

1) Models and algorithms
2) Software 
3) Compute

Our full-stack platform continuously optimizes for these inputs using extreme co-design across compute, networking, storage, and memory. Plus, software with broad ecosystem support across millions of developers.

The result: lower cost per token, higher throughput, and more scalable AI systems.

items.title:
Would you let your boss surveil you? 

Well, probably not if I pitched it to you like that.

But I'v...
items.content:
Would you let your boss surveil you? 

Well, probably not if I pitched it to you like that.

But I've now seen two companies that surveil you: they watch you work, watch you talk to people on Google Meet, answer your Slacks, and watch your email and your calendar. They turn you into superhumans, and they turn your company into a much more productive company.

So now, would you want that for your company? 

I just saw a stunning new company, and I'll have a video up tomorrow about that.

Years ago, I worked at Fast Company magazine, and this is a trend I would be all over. It would turn into a cover story on Fast Company.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer pitches AI workplace surveillance as beneficial - tools that watch workers' meetings, Slack, email, calendar to make them 'superhuman' and companies more productive. Teases upcoming video about a 'stunning new company.'
items.reasoning: Provocative take on AI-powered workplace monitoring. Good engagement, touches on important ethical questions about AI surveillance framed as productivity. Scobleizer has industry reach.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer pitches AI workplace surveillance as beneficial - tools that watch workers' meetings, Slack, email, calendar to make them 'superhuman' and companies more productive. Teases upcoming video about a 'stunning new company.'
items.content_html:
Would you let your boss surveil you?

Well, probably not if I pitched it to you like that.

But I've now seen two companies that surveil you: they watch you work, watch you talk to people on Google Meet, answer your Slacks, and watch your email and your calendar. They turn you into superhumans, and they turn your company into a much more productive company.

So now, would you want that for your company?

I just saw a stunning new company, and I'll have a video up tomorrow about that.

Years ago, I worked at Fast Company magazine, and this is a trend I would be all over. It would turn into a cover story on Fast Company.

items.title: @esrtweet I did a Doom port on BeOS, and I found it very pleasant. The NeXT workstations we were usi...
items.content:
@esrtweet I did a Doom port on BeOS, and I found it very pleasant. The NeXT workstations we were using had all the 90s Unix cruft (terminfo, as you mentioned, was a good example even then), while BeOS seemed to have implemented just the core goodness.

In some ways, it might well have been a better transition OS for Apple at the time, but in the long run with the rise of Linux on the server, being “actually unix” with OSX genuinely has been the better path.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack shares experience porting Doom to BeOS, comparing it favorably to NeXT Unix systems, and reflecting on Apple's choice of NeXT/OSX being the better long-term path due to Linux's server dominance.
items.reasoning: High engagement (24K views, 411 likes) from legendary game developer. Historical tech insight but not AI-related. Interesting computing history from a primary source.
items.summary_html: John Carmack shares experience porting Doom to BeOS, comparing it favorably to NeXT Unix systems, and reflecting on Apple's choice of NeXT/OSX being the better long-term path due to Linux's server dominance.
items.content_html:
@esrtweet I did a Doom port on BeOS, and I found it very pleasant. The NeXT workstations we were using had all the 90s Unix cruft (terminfo, as you mentioned, was a good example even then), while BeOS seemed to have implemented just the core goodness.

In some ways, it might well have been a better transition OS for Apple at the time, but in the long run with the rise of Linux on the server, being “actually unix” with OSX genuinely has been the better path.

items.title: For AIMIP, models forecast the global atmosphere over 1979–2024, using historical data from 1979–201...
items.content:
For AIMIP, models forecast the global atmosphere over 1979–2024, using historical data from 1979–2014 for training and leaving the final decade held out for testing.

The benchmark focuses on the atmosphere alone, and leaves model architecture choices up to each submitter.

items.title:
The future of AI agents isn't text. It's a war room.

Hermes already lets you run a fleet of special...
items.content:
The future of AI agents isn't text. It's a war room.

Hermes already lets you run a fleet of specialized agents that hand work to each other.
 
The problem was visibility.
 
You had to SSH into your machine and grep logs to see what each one was doing.
 
Orchestration War Room is a browser dashboard sitting on top of that fleet.
 
You hire a leader, brief them, watch the team execute.
 
The floor shows operatives as avatars with callsigns and thought bubbles.
 
Animated arrows show delegation between them in real time.
 
A live kanban tracks every task across Todo, Ready, Running, Blocked.
 
From the UI you can:
 
> Hire new agents
> Retrain identity and skills
> Fire any operative instantly
 
It reads the native task database directly. Updates stream through server-sent events per mission.
 
The wiring stays inside the agent runtime.
 
Only the glass floor is new.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Describes 'Orchestration War Room', a browser dashboard for managing multi-agent fleets in the Hermes framework. Features include agent visualization as avatars, real-time delegation arrows, live kanban tracking, and abilities to hire/retrain/fire agents from UI.
items.reasoning: Interesting multi-agent orchestration UI concept but low engagement suggests limited impact. The visualization approach is novel but the implementation details suggest early-stage.
items.summary_html: Describes 'Orchestration War Room', a browser dashboard for managing multi-agent fleets in the Hermes framework. Features include agent visualization as avatars, real-time delegation arrows, live kanban tracking, and abilities to hire/retrain/fire agents from UI.
items.content_html:
The future of AI agents isn't text. It's a war room.

Hermes already lets you run a fleet of specialized agents that hand work to each other.

The problem was visibility.

You had to SSH into your machine and grep logs to see what each one was doing.

Orchestration War Room is a browser dashboard sitting on top of that fleet.

You hire a leader, brief them, watch the team execute.

The floor shows operatives as avatars with callsigns and thought bubbles.

Animated arrows show delegation between them in real time.

A live kanban tracks every task across Todo, Ready, Running, Blocked.

From the UI you can:

> Hire new agents

> Retrain identity and skills

> Fire any operative instantly

It reads the native task database directly. Updates stream through server-sent events per mission.

The wiring stays inside the agent runtime.

Only the glass floor is new.

more safety enables more responsibility

items.title: I've seen a lot of people offer you to invest in private companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX, S...
items.content:
I've seen a lot of people offer you to invest in private companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX, Stripe etc and you should really avoid these offers I think

It's always like "yeah bro I know a way to invest I'll DM"

Then it always turns out be some 3 or more layered SPV (special purpose vehicle)

Which is essentially the original person who invested in them wants to sell his stock but legally can't (it requires board approval to sell and they usually won't allow you to, called right of first refusal (ROFR)) so instead they set up an SPV which they sign a contract with to pay it whatever the shares are worth at exit

But then you read the fine print and there's 3 layers of SPVs who take % management fees and a large % of the gains

So even if their stock goes up, you barely profit from it because the fees are so insane

That is of course if you profit at all as these companies will mark the shares as void and worthless if they find out

So yes it's mostly a scam/racket you should stay away from unless you get a really good deal and read the fine print!

You should always try invest directly by asking, they probably say no but you can always ask! I ask staff or founders on DM when I am talking to them about a bug or feature idea for example, it helps to have followers but even if you don't you can always ask

Especially if you're influential in a specific topic, many startups love to have you invest but you have to be early usually

Anyway avoid SPVs or at least read the fine print!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio warns extensively about layered SPV investment scams for investing in private companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX
items.reasoning: Useful warning about investment scams in AI company shares. High engagement (127K views). Tangentially AI-relevant as it concerns investing in AI companies.

But then you read the fine print and there's 3 layers of SPVs who take % management fees and a large % of the gains

So even if their stock goes up, you barely profit from it because the fees are so insane

You should always try invest directly by asking, they probably say no but you can always ask! I ask staff or founders on DM when I am talking to them about a bug or feature idea for example, it helps to have followers but even if you don't you can always ask

items.title:
@mignano This will be an interesting problem.

Go to Grok. Ask it "please simulate a conversation be...
items.content:
@mignano This will be an interesting problem.

Go to Grok. Ask it "please simulate a conversation between Michael Mignano and Robert Scoble about the future of personal data." And it will. Very well. It knows me VERY DEEPLY. 

That is scary. But on the other hand, now my Agents know me very deeply and can serve me. 

More and more people will figure out that is very powerful. 

Others will try to keep themselves out of the AI. Which means they won't get that power, and will have to use other people's agents that they created.

My agent created https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ BECAUSE it knows me so deeply.

items.title:
Battery life is a solved problem with Framework 13 Pro running on Intel Panther Lake and Omarchy.

I...
items.content:
Battery life is a solved problem with Framework 13 Pro running on Intel Panther Lake and Omarchy.

I took the laptop on my second trip and here are the numbers:

• I charged it before leaving home.
• I used it for 1.5 hours at the airport.
• Then, used it for about 5 hours during the flight.
• Then, used it for about 1 hour the next morning.

I plugged the laptop with 36%.

My usage was mostly browsing the web and social media, about 1 hour of Claude Code and Visual Studio Code, and I had a docker container running a database all the time.

Honestly, this is more battery life than what I need.

I can leave my house early on without carrying a charger and know I'll be back in the afternoon with enough juice.

Most are just scaling chaos.

items.content:
Everyone thinks they’re scaling AI.

Most are just scaling chaos.

What I’m seeing, including in conversations with @collibra, is a dangerous illusion

→ faster experimentation
→ more pilots
→ zero control

The gap between AI ambition and actual business value is widening.

Here’s what’s really going on…

#CollibraPartner

items.title:
The real bottleneck isn’t models.

It’s unstructured data.

I saw a side-by-side demo that made this...
items.content:
The real bottleneck isn’t models.

It’s unstructured data.

I saw a side-by-side demo that made this obvious:

→ Raw data environment
• lower accuracy
• higher risk
• inefficient outputs

→ Metadata-enriched environment
• better decisions
• faster results
• measurable value

Same AI. Completely different outcomes.

The difference? Governance and structure.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon argues the real AI bottleneck is unstructured data, not models, comparing raw vs metadata-enriched environments.
items.reasoning: Part of a sponsored thread but makes a valid point about data quality vs model quality in enterprise AI.

items.title:
I gave an interview yesterday about how I'd recommend people to get started with AI today.

Here is ...
items.content:
I gave an interview yesterday about how I'd recommend people to get started with AI today.

Here is what I said:

1. Think of something you want to exist
2. Find an AI app builder
3. Start building

Runnable is a great option. You can use it to make websites, applications, presentations, you name it.

Today, they are running a contest, so you can even win some prizes with your idea.

If you needed more motivation, I hope this is it.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago shares advice on getting started with AI: think of something, find an AI app builder, start building - promotes Runnable
items.reasoning: Promotional content with basic advice.
items.summary_html: Santiago shares advice on getting started with AI: think of something, find an AI app builder, start building - promotes Runnable
items.content_html:
I gave an interview yesterday about how I'd recommend people to get started with AI today.

Here is what I said:

1. Think of something you want to exist

2. Find an AI app builder

3. Start building

Runnable is a great option. You can use it to make websites, applications, presentations, you name it.

Today, they are running a contest, so you can even win some prizes with your idea.

If you needed more motivation, I hope this is it.

items.content:
Here’s the bottom line:

AI won’t be won by who moves fastest.
It’ll be won by who governs best.

If your data isn’t structured, contextualized, and controlled, scaling AI will only amplify problems.

I break this down in detail in my latest video with Collibra.

Check out the full article: https://t.co/G6PX8FzxjA 

Or Comment or DM “GOVERNANCE” and I’ll share a practical setup tailored to your organization - so you can define the right governance model before you scale.

items.title:
The old limitation was not clarity.

AI voices were already clean and understandable.

The real limi...
items.content:
The old limitation was not clarity.

AI voices were already clean and understandable.

The real limitation was control.

Most AI speech sounded like it was reading a script.

Now, with audio tags, we can guide how the AI speaks, not just what it says.

That changes the creative ceiling.

items.title:
The biggest misconception?

Speed = progress.

In reality:

→ Teams are innovating faster than they ...
items.content:
The biggest misconception?

Speed = progress.

In reality:

→ Teams are innovating faster than they can govern
→ Outputs are inconsistent and hard to trust
→ Risk is increasing quietly in the background

Without a solid data foundation, AI lacks context.

And without context, AI is just… guesswork.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon warns that teams are innovating faster than they can govern, creating inconsistent and untrustworthy AI outputs.
items.reasoning: Part of sponsored Collibra thread. Generic enterprise AI governance talking points.

items.content_html:
The biggest misconception?

Speed = progress.

In reality:

→ Teams are innovating faster than they can govern

→ Outputs are inconsistent and hard to trust

→ Risk is increasing quietly in the background

Without a solid data foundation, AI lacks context.

And without context, AI is just… guesswork.

@MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts. items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses AI community being primarily on X/Twitter and how LinkedIn is used for first connections in SF.
items.reasoning: Meta-commentary about social platforms, not AI-relevant.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses AI community being primarily on X/Twitter and how LinkedIn is used for first connections in SF.
items.content_html:
@crystalwizard @0xJuliechen All I know is when I'm in San Francisco meeting somebody new, they always want to scan my LinkedIn code first. Then I ask them, "Are you on X?" because that's where I hang out.

Invariably, they tell me, "Oh, I rarely post on X," or "I don't post on X much," stuff like that. Then I talk them into joining X because the AI community is here.

It all depends on who you are, right?

items.title:
Here’s the problem with most AI setups:

→ Data is sent to the cloud
→ Models process remotely
→ Dec...
items.content:
Here’s the problem with most AI setups:

→ Data is sent to the cloud
→ Models process remotely
→ Decisions come back with delay

That delay is latency.

And in operations, latency slows performance, limits responsiveness, and impacts business value.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon explains the latency problem with cloud-based AI in operational settings.
items.reasoning: Part of T-Mobile sponsored thread. Basic edge computing argument.
items.summary_html: Van Loon explains the latency problem with cloud-based AI in operational settings.
items.content_html:
Here’s the problem with most AI setups:

→ Data is sent to the cloud

→ Models process remotely

→ Decisions come back with delay

That delay is latency.

And in operations, latency slows performance, limits responsiveness, and impacts business value.

items.title:
In real operations, speed isn’t a feature.
It’s the requirement.

Think about:
→ Manufacturing lines...
items.content:
In real operations, speed isn’t a feature.
It’s the requirement.

Think about:
→ Manufacturing lines
→ Logistics networks
→ Smart infrastructure

These environments depend on ultra-low latency and real-time responsiveness.

They don’t need insights later.
They need decisions now.

items.title:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLea...
items.content:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration! 

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people.    The future is here.  In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers:  Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes.   After summarizing all parts, you will receive comprehensive answers to your inquiries based on the consolidated summaries. By default, the initialized prompt will target essential points such as:  These inquiries are tailored for research articles within the computer science domain. Upon completion of the paper review, feel free to engage with the question() interface to ask further questions.

References 

Khan, A. A., Hasan, M. T., Kemell, K. K., Rasku, J., & Abrahamsson, P. (2025). Developing retrieval augmented generation (RAG) based LLM systems from PDFs: An experience report [Preprint]. arXiv. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YCGfBCNY4v

Santos, G. (2025, March 3). LLM + RAG: Creating an AI-powered file reader assistant. Towards Data Science. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YubIqDgyP8

items.title:
Singularity

The new data and empirical fresh evidence from NASA's James Webb Space Telescope sugges...
items.content:
Singularity

The new data and empirical fresh evidence from NASA's James Webb Space Telescope suggests we're an interior to singularity. This new theory means that most galaxies rotate in the same direction. This discovery adds new insight into the dynamics of galaxy formations and their evolution in the universe. It suggests a possible underlying symmetry or common influence affecting the rotational characteristics of these galaxies, which could lead to further understanding of cosmic structures and behaviors.

The theory says we're living inside a black hole, and the new study provides fresh evidence that our particular universe is the interior of a black hole. Let's see if this theory supports the foundational cosmology.

🔴 In standard cosmology, the universe's accelerating expansion is usually attributed to dark energy (or a cosmological constant). In this new theory of black hole cosmology models, the idea is that the interior solution of a black hole might resemble an expanding universe. From a conventional cosmological model, our universe is expanding at an accelerated rate. However, in this classic black hole cosmology, the acceleration couldn't form from a force inside a classical black hole but from the way the geometry of spacetime evolves inside this proposed solution. Essentially, the new study suggests that the black hole's interior has an effective metric that permits expansion—and even accelerated expansion—analogous to the role played by dark energy in our standard models.

🔴 If we assume our universe is the interior of a black hole, the "event horizon" may not serve as a strict boundary limiting internal processes the way it does in the standard model. In these models, the region inside the black hole can be reinterpreted as a dynamic, expanding space where the usual rules of structure formation apply. Galaxies form through gravitational collapse, and other processes in an evolving spacetime can rise from a big bang within a black hole. In this view, the birth of hundreds of billions of galaxies isn't a consequence of the black hole devouring matter from outside but rather a result of how absorbed matter organizes itself within an expanding, curved spacetime that was set up at the bounce or creation event that—by analogy—overriding the traditional Big Bang.

🔴 The new study, which looked at 263 galaxies, reported a statistical preference in spin direction.

🔴 Moreover, suppose this black hole cosmology model were correct. In that case, the interior metric might allow for certain spacetime features (such as a preferred rotation or other anisotropies) that emerge from its specific initial conditions. However, the prevailing view supported by numerous observations is that the universe does not have a preferred direction on large scale. More data—especially from JWST and other upcoming surveys—will help clarify whether these observed alignments are significant or just local quirks.

Open questions

🟦 The theory supports that initial empty space structure is a brute fact that always existed, like a mathematical zero that does not require an initialization, causality, reason, or logic. of the blackhole cosmological model birthing new universes.

🟦 How does the event horizon that acts as a barrier remain a boundary in this dynamic, expanding spacetime of the new universe? How does it avoid the collision with singularity? The matter transforms into another scale of geometry that creates spacetime. Can the blackhole cosmological model support the creation or reproduction of the cosmic microwave background, large-scale structure, and observed expansion history, or can it have its laws of physics governed within the interior?

🟦 We have not been able to integrate the complex mathematics of quantum mechanics with the classical black holes cosmology (and their interiors). This theory leaves us the new study with a model that is conceptually intriguing but mathematically incomplete.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

References 

Shamir, L. (2025, February 17). The distribution of galaxy rotation in JWST Advanced Deep Extragalactic Survey. Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society, 538(1), 76–83. https://t.co/JtmuainkoR. Retrieved March 19, 2025, from https://t.co/znQ5WnquQW

Cuthbertson, A. (2025, March 19). New NASA data hints we could be living inside a black hole. The Independent. Retrieved March 19, 2025, from https://t.co/ybCw9tsbjk

items.content:
Strategy 

Ph.D. in Business Administration 
Ph.D. in Management 
Ph.D. in Strategy 

AI Strategy 
AI Plan 
Govern AI 
Manage AI 
Secure AI 
AI Center of Excellence 

An AI technology strategy involves a systematic approach to integrating AI into your organization. Start by assessing your existing capabilities and identifying AI technologies that align with your team’s skills, the data you have access to, and your budget constraints. Microsoft provides various options like Software as a Service (SaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), each offering distinct levels of customization and shared responsibility.

To effectively choose the right AI tools and platforms, consider utilizing an AI decision tree that evaluates your specific needs, objectives, and resources. The decision tree can help clarify which technologies would best support your organization's goals, facilitate data management, and enhance overall efficiency. Additionally, review guidance materials that offer insight into best practices and potential pitfalls in AI implementation. This structured approach ensures a well-informed and strategic adoption of AI technologies within your organization.

Glean’s platform seamlessly integrates with a wide array of enterprise systems such as email, intranet, cloud storage, and database by providing a powerful, unified search interface with AI Agents that effortlessly retrieves information from all connected sources. This comprehensive solution empowers users to access critical data quickly and efficiently, enhancing productivity and informed decision-making across your organization. items.title:
Mega Messiah -  St. Francis of Paola's Day 

On April 2, Catholics honor St. Francis of Paola, a rem...
items.content:
Mega Messiah -  St. Francis of Paola's Day 

On April 2, Catholics honor St. Francis of Paola, a remarkable figure who founded a transformative religious order at a young age from Italy. In a time marked by corruption within the Church, he passionately sought to revive the authentic practices of the earliest monks, embodying a spiritual renaissance that continues to inspire devotion and reform today. 

Francis Paola was born 600+ years ago in the picturesque Southern Italian region of Calabria. His parents, deeply devoted to St. Francis of Assisi, chose to name their son in honor of the beloved saint. Although they lacked material wealth, they endowed Francis with a profound spiritual legacy, nurturing the hope that he would follow in the footsteps of his namesake and embody the virtues of humility and devotion. 

From a young age, Francis displayed profound spiritual depth, embracing poverty as a means to holiness just like his parents. At the age of 13, when his father entrusted him to a group of Franciscan friars for his education, Francis made a pivotal personal commitment to live strictly according to the rule of their religious order. This decision marked the beginning of a remarkable journey dedicated to faith, selflessness, and devotion.
